Equity Research · Initiation of Coverage
Industrials — Building Products
2026-06-29
Initiation of Coverage

Owens Corning

NYSE: OC
InitiationBuilding ProductsNYSE: OCFair Value

Executive Summary

Owens Corning is a high-quality, North America-centric building-products manufacturer that, after a strong rally to near its 52-week high, is now fairly valued rather than the bargain it looked at its lows. On a DCF terminal anchored to OC's own through-cycle EV/EBITDA - a 7.5x exit, the upper half of its realized 4.5-8.0x range - intrinsic value is $156/share, level with the price. The market's headline discount to building-products peers (8.6x vs ~14x) is real but structural: OC has persistently traded several turns below the higher-quality, less-cyclical names, so it is not a re-rating catalyst. The case for owning OC now rests on cash quality and optionality - a sector-high ~6.6% FCF yield, self-funded deleveraging to ~1.0x, and a potential Doors-margin/housing recovery - most of which is already reflected in today's 8.6x. With no valuation cushion and the stock technically extended, the stance is to wait for a pullback.

Financial Snapshot↑

Investment Thesis↑

  • Fairly valued on its own history: the 7.5x-exit DCF base ($156) equals the price; a through-cycle-average multiple (~6-6.5x) implies ~$123-140, and only 9-10x (above anything OC has closed a year at) reaches the $190+ "cheap" zone.
  • Structural, not closable, peer discount: 8.6x vs ~14x peers reflects OC's higher cyclicality, ~2.5x leverage and the margin-dilutive Doors mix - a persistent feature, not a catalyst.
  • Cash quality is the real attraction: a sector-high ~6.6% FCF yield (+225bps over the 10-yr Treasury), with FCF building from ~$0.82bn to ~$1.3bn as the FY2026 capex peak rolls off.
  • Self-funded deleveraging: net leverage falls from ~2.5x toward ~1.0x by FY2030E while funding ~$1bn/yr of dividends and buybacks - both equity and credit improve through the cycle.
  • Optionality, not base case: a Doors-margin recovery (11% -> mid-teens) and a stronger housing/R&R upcycle drive the bull case (~$216); neither is required for fair value, and neither is proven.

Key Strengths↑

  • Top-two share in all three core markets (roofing, insulation, doors), defended by scale, vertical integration (own asphalt + glass mat) and the PINK(R) brand.
  • Structurally high, resilient margins - ~22% Adjusted EBITDA held through a volume downturn on cost-out, pricing and mix.
  • Strong, durable cash conversion funding a 12-year record of dividend growth plus consistent buybacks.
  • Oligopolistic, capital-intensive end-markets with high entry barriers and code/spec-driven differentiation.

Key Risks↑

  • Valuation at the top of its range: at 8.6x OC is above its FY2021-25 year-end multiples; reversion toward the ~6-7x through-cycle average implies $123-145 - downside to the current price.
  • No margin of safety: intrinsic value ~= price on the base case, and the DCF is ~74% terminal value.
  • Housing/R&R cyclicality and rate sensitivity - a deeper downturn drives the bear case (~$86).
  • Doors/Masonite: a ~$3.9bn deal already impaired $1.2bn; the margin recovery is unproven and central to the return on deal capital.

Competitive Positioning↑

OC occupies the branded-manufacturer node - the richest, most stable margin pool in its value chains - and is more vertically integrated than most peers, producing its own oxidized asphalt and glass mat. It is the #2 U.S. asphalt-shingle producer (behind privately held GAF), North America's largest fiberglass-insulation producer, and a top door producer via Masonite, competing against deep-pocketed rivals (Saint-Gobain, Knauf, Johns Manville, JELD-WEN, Fortune Brands). It carries a higher Adjusted margin than the peer median yet trades several turns below them on EV/EBITDA - but that discount is long-standing and structural, reflecting cyclicality and the Doors drag rather than a clear mispricing.

Entry Verdict↑

OC is Fair Value - the 7.5x-exit DCF ($156) sits level with the price, and the wide peer discount is structural rather than a catalyst. With no valuation cushion and the shares technically extended (overbought, RSI 72, ~29% above a rising 100-day EMA near the 52-week high), the stance is Wait: prefer a pullback toward the EMA100 (~$121-140), which would restore both a margin of safety below the $156 base and a cleaner technical entry. The bull case (~$216) needs an operational upturn or re-rating; the bear (~$86) is a cycle trough.

Fair Value — wait for a pullback (no valuation cushion)

1Company Overview↑ Contents

Key takeaways

  • High-quality, branded building-products manufacturer: OC converts asphalt, glass and wood into market-leading shingles, insulation and doors, earning a ~22% Adjusted-EBITDA margin on scale, vertical integration and brand — a genuinely high-quality industrial, not a commodity processor.
  • The central debate is the Masonite/Doors bet: the $3.9bn deal has been impaired $1.2bn and the segment earns just ~11% margins; the thesis rests on durable Roofing economics (proven) plus a Doors-margin recovery (aspirational, the swing factor).
  • Defining risk is cyclicality + concentration: earnings are highly levered to U.S. housing/R&R and weather, with a combined Chair/CEO and a sole-source Roofing input adding governance and supply tail-risks.

1.1Company Description↑

Owens Corning is a North America–centric manufacturer of branded residential and commercial building products, selling shingles, insulation and doors through distributors, home centers and contractors. It makes money by converting asphalt, glass and wood/composite inputs into branded building materials at scale, leveraging vertical integration (it produces its own glass mat and asphalt) and market-leading positions to earn structurally high margins.

Founded in 1938 and headquartered in Toledo, Ohio, OC operates three reportable segments — Roofing, Insulation and Doors — across ~126 continuing-operations manufacturing facilities, with ~25,000 employees in 31 countries. The company is mid-way through a deliberate portfolio reshaping: it acquired Masonite (the Doors platform) for ~$3.9bn in May 2024 and, in February 2025, agreed to divest its Glass Reinforcements (GR) business (~$474m, closed early 2026), narrowing OC to a focused North American/European building-products company.

  • FY2025 net sales: $10,103m (+2.6% YoY); LTM (through 2026-Q1) revenue: $9,838m.
  • FY2025 adjusted EBITDA: $2,268m → 22.4% margin (company-defined adjusted EBITDA, continuing operations).
  • FY2025 reported a net loss of $(522)m (and $(188)m from continuing operations) on $1.2bn of non-cash goodwill/intangible impairments tied to the Doors business.

Read-through: The headline GAAP loss is impairment-driven, not a cash or operating-collapse event — OC still generated $962m of free cash flow in FY2025 at a 22% adjusted-EBITDA margin. The investment debate is less about FY2025 solvency and more about whether the $3.9bn Masonite bet was value-destructive and whether Roofing's margins are durable.

1.2Product Offering↑

OC sells branded building products into the residential repair-and-remodel (R&R), new-residential-construction and commercial-construction channels, packaged as systems (e.g., roofing components sold alongside shingles; insulation "systems solutions").

  • Roofing: laminate asphalt shingles, roofing components, underlayment, oxidized asphalt, composite lumber, and glass mat/specialty veil — e.g., the Duration® shingle line (owenscorning.com/en-us/roofing).
  • Insulation: fiberglass batts and loosefill under the PINK® Next Gen™ FIBERGLAS™ brand, plus FOAMULAR® XPS, FOAMGLAS® cellular glass and Paroc® stone wool (owenscorning.com/en-us/insulation).
  • Doors: interior and exterior residential doors and door systems (wood, glass, fiberglass, metal) and components, via the Masonite platform (masonite.com).

1.3Key Reporting Segments↑

OC recast to three reportable segments effective January 1, 2025 (removing GR to discontinued operations). Shares below are of FY2025 segment net sales and segment EBITDA; margins are calculated (segment EBITDA / segment net sales).

Segment Description & FY2025 economics
Roofing — 43.2% of revenue · 56.7% of segment EBITDA · 31.8% EBITDA margin Laminate asphalt shingles plus roofing components and oxidized asphalt; #2 U.S. asphalt-shingle producer, vertically integrated in asphalt and glass mat. Demand is R&R-led and storm-driven, so it does not strictly follow new-construction cycles — the highest-margin, most cash-generative leg.
Insulation — 36.1% of revenue · 34.0% of segment EBITDA · 22.9% EBITDA margin Residential and technical fiberglass, foam and stone-wool insulation; North America's largest fiberglass-insulation producer with a Canada/Europe/Latin America footprint. Levered to housing starts (≈3-month lag), R&R, non-residential and tightening energy codes — solid mid-20s margins with a secular efficiency tailwind.
Doors — 20.7% of revenue · 9.3% of segment EBITDA · 10.9% EBITDA margin Interior/exterior residential doors and systems (Masonite), one of North America's largest door producers. Most exposed to new-residential construction and the weakest-margin segment; FY2025 EBITDA was flat YoY despite a full year of consolidation, and it absorbed the $1.2bn impairment.

Roofing contributes 43% of revenue but 57% of segment EBITDA (above-portfolio margin), while Doors is 21% of revenue yet only 9% of EBITDA (margin-dilutive) — the segment mix is the central margin story.

1.4Geographies↑

OC is overwhelmingly a North American business, with a secondary European footprint and a negligible Asia-Pacific/rest-of-world presence. Net sales are attributed by the location from which product is sold.

  • FY2025 net sales by region: North America $8,853m (87.6%), Europe $1,152m (11.4%), Asia-Pacific $68m (0.7%), Rest of world $30m (0.3%).
  • Mix shift: the North America share has been broadly stable (86.1% FY2023 → 87.6% FY2025); Europe has held ~11–12% while Asia-Pacific has shrunk (from $154m FY2023 to $68m FY2025) as OC exited building-materials operations in China/Korea and reduced GR-related Asian exposure.
  • Geographic concentration risk: earnings are highly levered to U.S. housing and R&R cycles, U.S. interest rates and U.S. weather (storm-driven roofing demand). Europe adds modest diversification (mainly Insulation/Paroc), and management cited European market improvement plus FX tailwinds as a 2026 swing factor.
Region FY2023 % FY2025 % FY2025 $m
North America 86.1% 87.6% 8,853
Europe 11.8% 11.4% 1,152
Asia-Pacific 1.8% 0.7% 68
Rest of world 0.2% 0.3% 30

1.5Demand Drivers↑

Demand is driven by U.S. residential R&R, U.S. new-residential construction, U.S./European non-residential construction, and energy-efficiency regulation — each tied to a quantifiable end market.

  • Residential R&R (largest exposure): U.S. homeowner improvement spending is projected at ~$522bn in 2026, but growth is decelerating to ~1.6% by Q4 2026 (Harvard JCHS LIRA, Jan 2026, accessed 2026-06-29). Drives Roofing (re-roofing) and Insulation R&R demand — secular (aging housing stock) with a cyclical overlay.
  • New residential construction: U.S. single-family starts are projected roughly flat in 2026 (Builders FirstSource 2026 outlook, accessed 2026-06-29). Most important to Doors and to Insulation (≈3-month lag to starts) — cyclical, rate-sensitive.
  • Storm activity: major-storm roof damage can sharply lift Roofing demand independent of construction cycles — cyclical/weather; lower storm carryover hurt Q4 2025.
  • Energy codes / efficiency: tightening building codes and decarbonization sustain insulation intensity per home — secular/regulatory tailwind for Insulation.

1.6Cost Drivers↑

The dominant variable costs are raw materials and energy; the business is also capital- and fixed-cost-intensive (143 plants), so volumes drive operating leverage.

  • Key inputs: oxidized asphalt (Roofing; correlated to crude oil), glass (sand/cullet and energy for melting), wood and composite materials (Doors), plus freight and natural gas/electricity for energy-intensive melting/curing. One Roofing raw material is sole-sourced under a long-term contract (single-source risk; see Suppliers).
  • Vertical integration partly insulates margins: OC produces its own oxidized asphalt and the glass mat used in shingles (mat uses wet-use chopped strand from Insulation).
  • Margin movers: selling price vs. input-cost spread, plant utilization/volumes, and product mix. FY2025 adjusted EBITDA margin held at 22% despite lower volumes, which management attributes to structural cost-out, favorable mix and pricing. Environmental compliance opex was ~$78m in FY2025. A precise fixed/variable cost split is not disclosed (gap).

1.7AI Considerations↑

AI is a modest operational opportunity rather than a disruption threat for OC — building-products manufacturing and physical distribution are not directly substitutable by software.

  • Opportunity: manufacturing-process optimization (yield, energy use in glass melting, predictive maintenance across 126 plants), demand forecasting/inventory management, and pricing analytics — incremental margin/efficiency levers consistent with the structural cost-out program.
  • Risk: limited and indirect — e.g., AI-enabled competitor cost advantage or changes in homebuilder design/procurement workflows. No evidence of a near-term substitution threat to shingles, insulation or doors (analyst view; OC does not break out AI initiatives in filings — gap).

1.8Research & Development↑

OC's R&D ("science and technology") spend is modest as a share of sales but rising, consistent with a process- and product-innovation (not R&D-intensive) business model.

  • S&T expense: $150m FY2025 (1.5% of sales), $131m FY2024 (1.3%). Trend is upward in dollars.
  • Versus peers: broadly in line with building-products manufacturers, which typically spend ~1–2% of sales on R&D (analyst view — gap). Innovation centers on product performance (longer-life laminate shingles, higher-R-value/lower-embodied-carbon insulation).

1.9Suppliers↑

Key supply inputs are asphalt/crude derivatives, glass raw materials, energy and wood/composites; specific named suppliers are not disclosed.

  • Concentration / single-source risk: the FY2025 10-K flags that one raw material important to Roofing is sourced from a sole supplier (under a long-term contract) and that asphalt supply has been periodically constricted — both genuine supply-chain risks that could interrupt Roofing production if disrupted.
  • Geographic/geopolitical: tariffs/trade policy are a watch item; management estimated a ~$10m Q1-2026 net tariff impact, characterized as minimal under current policy.

1.10Employees↑

OC had ~25,000 total employees at December 31, 2025, of which ~11,000 (≈44%) were located outside the United States.

  • Unionization: ~61% of total hourly employees are covered by collective bargaining agreements — a meaningful labor-relations exposure. No specific upcoming CBA renegotiations or recent disputes are disclosed (gap).
  • Mix: a function/segment/geography headcount split is not disclosed beyond the U.S./ex-U.S. cut.

1.11Key IP or Facilities↑

OC's most valuable intangible asset is brand — most notably the PINK® single-color trademark (first U.S. single-color trademark, in use since 1956) and the Owens Corning, FOAMULAR®, FOAMGLAS®, Paroc® and Masonite brands — supported by a patent portfolio that the company states is not dependent on any single right.

  • Facilities (Dec 31, 2025): 143 manufacturing facilities total (126 continuing operations + 17 in GR/discontinued), of which 103 are owned. Continuing-ops plants by segment: Roofing 41, Insulation 36, Doors 49 (89 in the U.S., 17 in Europe, 20 rest-of-world).
  • HQ / R&D: owned World Headquarters in Toledo, Ohio (~400,000 sq ft) and the owned Science & Technology Center near Granville, Ohio (~500 acres, 650,000+ sq ft).

1.12Key Acquisitions and Divestitures↑

OC is actively reshaping its portfolio toward focused North American/European building products, most-recent-first:

  • Glass Reinforcements (GR) divestiture — announced Feb 13, 2025; closed early 2026. Sale of the global GR business for ~$436m at signing (~$474m net of cash, less costs to sell, at Dec 31, 2025). Classified as discontinued operations from Jan 1, 2025. Rationale: exit lower-margin, more-industrial/cyclical composites to focus on building products.
  • China/Korea building-materials business — sold during 2025, part of the strategic narrowing.
  • Masonite International — acquired May 15, 2024 for ~$3.9bn ($133.00/share cash, ~38% premium) (Owens Corning newsroom, accessed 2026-06-29). Cash outflow net of cash acquired was $2,857m; funded with a term loan and new debt. Created the Doors segment; targeted ~$125m of run-rate cost synergies (now expected to be exceeded by mid-2026). Purchase multiple ≈ 8.6× EV/EBITDA (estimate; implied Masonite EBITDA not separately disclosed — gap). The business was subsequently impaired by $1.2bn in 2H 2025 — a clear negative read on deal value/timing.
  • Prior period: no other material acquisitions disclosed in FY2021–FY2023; pre-Masonite activity was primarily organic plus smaller bolt-ons.

1.13Collateral Overview↑

Working-capital and fixed-asset balances are substantial and asset-heavy, consistent with a capital-intensive manufacturer.

  • Receivables: $937m (Dec 31, 2025; net of $4m allowance) — down from $1,140m a year earlier on lower Q4 volumes.
  • Inventories: $1,492m (2026-Q1) / $1,472m (Dec 31, 2025) — building modestly.
  • PP&E, net: $4,121m (2026-Q1) / $4,170m (Dec 31, 2025) — the dominant collateral asset; capex ran ~$824m in FY2025 (above D&A).
  • Goodwill & intangibles: goodwill $1,664m and intangibles $2,498m (2026-Q1); goodwill fell from $2,745m (FY2024) on the Doors impairments.

1.14Management Overview↑

Leadership is long-tenured and largely internally promoted. Note the combined Chair/CEO role — Brian Chambers holds both, a governance feature worth monitoring (lower board independence at the top).

  • Brian D. Chambers (59) — Board Chair, President & CEO since April 2020 (CEO since 2019); rose through the Roofing/building-materials businesses; oversaw the Masonite acquisition and the portfolio reshaping.
  • Todd W. Fister (51) — EVP & CFO since September 2023; previously President of the Insulation segment — an internal finance/operations promotion.
  • Chairperson: same as CEO (no separate independent board chair; lead-director structure to be confirmed from the proxy — gap).
  • Background check: no material litigation, regulatory action, fraud or personal controversy involving the named CEO/CFO was identified in reputable third-party sources as of 2026-06-29 — no material issues identified.

1.15Other Risks / Comments↑

Company-specific risks beyond the industry-level risks in the Industry Overview:

  • Doors/Masonite impairment & integration risk: $1.2bn of non-cash impairments in 2H 2025 signal the ~$3.9bn deal is, so far, value-dilutive; the segment's EBITDA margin fell to 11% (from 16%). Synergy execution ($125m+ by mid-2026, plus ~$75m structural) is now central to the thesis.
  • Leverage: net debt ~$4.8bn at FY2025, ≈2.1× adjusted EBITDA — manageable but elevated post-Masonite; interest guided to $255–265m for 2026.
  • GAAP loss / negative TTM EPS: reported diluted EPS of $(6.22) FY2025 is impairment-driven but removes the trailing P/E as an anchor and may weigh on sentiment.
  • Capital-allocation scrutiny: ~$1.0bn returned in FY2025 ($232m dividends + $770m buybacks) and a 15% dividend raise — supportive, but funded alongside elevated capex and post-deal leverage.
  • Key-person / governance: combined Chair/CEO; a 2025 reshuffle of all three segment presidents adds near-term execution-continuity risk.

2Industry Overview↑ Contents

Key takeaways

  • Structurally advantaged in consolidated, capital-intensive markets: OC holds top-two share in three oligopolistic sub-markets (roofing, insulation, doors) where scale, vertical integration, freight economics and code/brand approvals create real barriers — it sits in the highest-margin (branded-manufacturer) node of the value chain.
  • The growth pool is low-to-mid-single-digit but durable: re-roofing/R&R (non-discretionary, ~20–30-yr replacement) and tightening energy codes underpin ~4–5% roofing and secular insulation demand, partly offsetting the cyclicality of new-construction-levered Doors.
  • Margin defense rests on pricing discipline: oligopoly structure and brand pull let OC raise Roofing/Insulation prices even in a down-volume year; the clearest vulnerability is cyclical volume deleverage plus the lower-margin Doors mix.

2.1Industry Definition, Size & Characteristics↑

OC competes in North American building products, resolved into three distinct sub-industries: (1) asphalt roofing (shingles and components), (2) building insulation (fiberglass, foam, stone wool), and (3) residential doors (interior/exterior and systems). Adjacent-but-out-of-scope categories include windows, siding, HVAC, gypsum and cement, and — following the GR divestiture — industrial glass-fiber composites.

  • Market size (base year ~2024–2025): U.S. asphalt shingles ≈ $7.4bn (Mordor Intelligence / Freedonia); U.S. insulation products ≈ $13bn (Market Research Future); North America residential doors ≈ $9.7bn (Expert Market Research). Combined, OC addresses a multi-tens-of-billions opportunity, with leadership positions in all three.
  • Character: cyclical, consolidated, capital-intensive and differentiated-by-brand/scale. Demand tracks housing and R&R cycles, but each sub-market is dominated by a handful of vertically integrated, scale players, with high plant capital intensity and freight economics creating regional moats. These are not pure commodities — brand (PINK®), code compliance, distribution access and product systems create real differentiation.

2.2Structural Trends & End-Market Demand↑

Near-term demand is soft-but-stabilizing (rate-pressured new construction, decelerating R&R), while the multi-year structural backdrop — aging housing stock, energy-efficiency regulation and re-roofing necessity — supports low-to-mid-single-digit market growth.

  • Asphalt roofing: projected ~4.9–5.6% CAGR to 2030 (Mordor; Market Data Forecast). Demand is dominated by re-roofing/R&R plus storm restoration — a largely secular + weather driver that makes Roofing the most defensive end market OC serves.
  • Insulation: levered to new residential construction (≈3-month lag), R&R, non-residential, and tightening energy codes — a secular/regulatory growth vector layered on a cyclical base.
  • Doors: most exposed to new-residential construction and big-ticket remodeling — the most cyclical, rate-sensitive end market; NA residential doors are projected to grow ~3.9% CAGR to 2034 (Expert Market Research).
  • Demand backdrop into 2026: U.S. homeowner R&R spending ≈ $522bn in 2026 but decelerating from ~2.9% (Q1) to ~1.6% (Q4) (Harvard JCHS LIRA); single-family starts roughly flat (Builders FirstSource), with R&R expected to re-accelerate ahead of new construction into 2027.
  • Technology change: incremental, not disruptive — cool-roof/algae-resistant shingles, low-embodied-carbon insulation, automation in door fabrication. No substitution threat to the core categories is evident.

2.3Industry Structure & Value Chain↑

Each sub-market is moderately-to-highly consolidated with high barriers to entry: heavy plant capital, scale-driven raw-material and freight economics, code/spec approvals, brand and entrenched distribution.

  • Concentration: U.S. asphalt shingles is effectively an oligopoly led by GAF (Standard Industries), Owens Corning and CertainTeed (Saint-Gobain) (Mordor). Fiberglass insulation is similarly consolidated — the top five producers hold >40% (Reports and Data). Precise CR4/HHI figures are not reliably published (gap). Doors is more fragmented but led by a few national brands.
  • Value chain (upstream → downstream): raw materials (crude/asphalt, glass sand, wood/composites, energy) → manufacturing (OC's position) → distribution (two-step distributors, home centers, lumberyards) → contractors/installers → homeowner/builder. OC sits in the manufacturing margin pool and is vertically integrated upstream, capturing more of the chain than non-integrated peers. The richest, most stable margins sit at the branded-manufacturer node and in re-roofing; distribution/installation are lower-margin and more fragmented.

2.4Competitive Landscape & Positioning↑

OC holds top-two positions in all three of its core markets, a genuinely strong competitive position, though it faces deep-pocketed, vertically integrated rivals in each.

  • Roofing: #2 U.S. asphalt-shingle producer (company estimate), behind privately held GAF/Standard Industries and ahead of/alongside CertainTeed (Saint-Gobain), IKO, TAMKO, Malarkey (Holcim).
  • Insulation: North America's largest fiberglass-insulation producer, competing with Knauf Insulation, Johns Manville (Berkshire Hathaway), CertainTeed (Saint-Gobain) and, in stone wool, Rockwool.
  • Doors: one of North America's largest door producers (via Masonite), against JELD-WEN and Therma-Tru (Fortune Brands Innovations) (Expert Market Research).
  • Share trend: reported share positions are management estimates; independently verified percentages by sub-segment are not reliably disclosed (gap) — the qualitative read is leadership held, with no clear evidence of share gain or loss in FY2025.
  • Value proposition: OC's edge is scale + vertical integration + brand + multi-category distribution access; the recent strategic logic is to cross-sell roofing, insulation and doors through "The OC Advantage" integrated go-to-market.

2.5Margin Dynamics: Bargaining Power & Key Constraints↑

The investment-relevant power dynamics are (1) manufacturer pricing power vs. large retail/distribution customers, (2) input-cost (asphalt/energy) pass-through, and (3) supply-side constraints in roofing.

  • Customer bargaining power (moderate, rising risk): big-box home centers and large two-step distributors are powerful buyers, but the oligopolistic supply side and brand pull preserve manufacturer pricing power — evidenced by OC realizing higher Roofing/Insulation prices in FY2025 even as volumes fell. Customer concentration "in certain areas" is flagged but unquantified (gap).
  • Supplier power / input pass-through: asphalt costs track crude oil and are passed through with a lag, so margins compress when crude spikes faster than list prices reset, and benefit when crude falls. Energy (natural gas/electricity for glass melting) is a key swing cost.
  • Key supply constraint (roofing): OC relies on a sole-source supplier for one important Roofing raw material and notes periodic asphalt-supply tightness — a genuine, if contracted, bottleneck.
  • Net read: the branded-manufacturer node captures a durable margin pool (mid-30s% in Roofing, low-20s% in Insulation), defended by scale, integration and consolidation. The clearest vulnerability is cyclical volume deleverage and the structurally lower-margin Doors business diluting the mix.

3Financial Overview↑ Contents

Continuing-operations basis (ex–Glass Reinforcements, divested; closed 30 Apr 2026). USD, US GAAP, FY-end December. LTM = trailing twelve months through 1Q26. Headline non-GAAP = company-reported Adjusted EBITDA & Adjusted EPS. FY2026E–FY2030E are model estimates. Read with a dual equity + leveraged-credit lens.

Comparability flag — two bases, broken at FY2023→FY2024. FY2021–FY2023 are on the as-reported total-company basis (including the former Composites/GR business and pre-Masonite); FY2024–FY2025 are on the recast continuing-operations basis (ex-GR, post-Masonite; Roofing/Insulation/Doors). The FY2023→FY2024 step (reported revenue $9.68bn → $9.85bn) reflects the Masonite acquisition and the GR divestiture/discontinued-ops recast, not an organic move; growth across that boundary is not like-for-like.

Key takeaways

  • Equity — durable margins, FCF inflection ahead: a ~22% Adjusted-EBITDA margin held through a volume downturn, and FCF rebuilds from ~$0.82bn (FY2026E) to ~$1.3bn (FY2030E) as the FY2026 capex peak rolls off; ROIC (~13–14%) already exceeds the ~8.5% WACC, so the franchise is value-creating on an adjusted basis.
  • Credit — clear, self-funded deleveraging: net leverage falls from ~2.5x (LTM) toward ~1.0x by FY2030E with interest coverage rising to ~13x; FCF/Adjusted-EBITDA conversion improves from ~40% toward ~50% as capex normalizes — a BBB-profile balance sheet on an improving path.
  • The watch item is FY2025's GAAP-vs-adjusted gap: the $(522)m net loss is non-cash (Doors impairment), so the thesis rests on adjusted/cash economics — Roofing pricing durability and Doors-margin recovery are the metrics that decide whether the deleveraging path holds.
($MM unless noted)FY21FY22FY23FY24FY25LTMFY26EFY27EFY28EFY29EFY30E

Financial summary

SegmentSegment DescriptionSegment Financials

3.1Revenue & segment trajectory↑

Revenue grew to $10.1bn in FY2025 (+2.6%), but the mix is the story: a full year of Doors masked volume softness, and Q4-2025/Q1-2026 showed the cyclical downturn (Q1-2026 revenue −10% YoY).

  • Roofing (~43% of revenue, ~32% EBITDA margin) is the franchise — #2 U.S. asphalt-shingle producer, R&R-led and storm-exposed, the highest-margin and most cash-generative leg.
  • Insulation (~36%, ~23% margin) is steady, levered to housing starts (≈3-month lag), R&R and energy codes.
  • Doors (~21%, ~11% margin) is the swing factor — most exposed to new-residential construction, EBITDA flat YoY in FY2025 despite a full year of consolidation, and the source of the impairment.
  • LTM revenue of ~$9.84bn reflects the soft start to 2026; segment EBITDA margins compressed across all three in 1Q26 (Roofing 24%, Insulation 19%, Doors 7%).

3.2Margins & profitability (equity lens)↑

Adjusted EBITDA margin of ~22% (FY2025) / ~21% (LTM) has proven resilient on lower volumes, supported by structural cost-out (>$125m Masonite synergies plus ~$75m structural), pricing and mix — but it sits below the company's mid-20s% long-term target.

  • GAAP operating income collapsed to $360m in FY2025 (from $1,482m) purely on the $1,174m of goodwill/intangible impairments; adjusted operating income (analyst-derived) was ~$1.57bn.
  • Reported diluted EPS was $(6.22) (FY2025) versus adjusted diluted EPS of $12.05 — the GAAP loss is non-cash and impairment-driven, so the trailing P/E is not a usable anchor.
  • Gross margin (~28%) is exposed to asphalt/crude and energy input swings, passed through with a lag.

Watch item: the gap between GAAP and adjusted earnings is unusually wide in FY2025; the thesis rests on adjusted/cash economics, so the durability of Roofing pricing and the pace of Doors-margin recovery are the metrics that matter.

3.3Cash flow & capital allocation↑

Cash conversion is the strongest part of the story: FY2025 operating cash flow $1.79bn and free cash flow $0.96bn (total company), funding ~$1.0bn of shareholder returns ($232m dividends + $770m buybacks) and a 15% dividend increase (12th consecutive year of growth).

  • Capex stepped up to $824m (FY2025) on network modernization — above D&A — a near-term FCF headwind that should normalize.
  • The Masonite acquisition ($2,857m net cash outflow in FY2024) was debt-funded and is the reason leverage rose; the GR divestiture (~$280m, closed Apr 2026) is a modest deleveraging input.
  • FCF conversion (FCF/adjusted EBITDA) of ~40–42% is depressed by elevated capex and should rise toward ~50% as capex normalizes.

3.4Balance sheet, leverage & coverage (credit lens)↑

Leverage is elevated post-Masonite but comfortably serviced and on a clear deleveraging path: net debt ~$4.8bn at FY2025, ≈2.1× adjusted EBITDA, with interest coverage ~8.9×.

  • Total debt ~$5.17bn vs. $345m cash at FY2025; 1Q26 net debt rose seasonally (~2.5× LTM) on working-capital build and a commercial-paper draw.
  • Working capital is asset-heavy but stable (DSO ~34, DIO ~74, DPO ~63; CCC ~45 days); PP&E (~$4.2bn) is the dominant collateral asset.
  • No near-term maturity wall is flagged; interest expense is guided to $255–265m for 2026.
  • Credit read: a BBB-profile manufacturer — high, resilient margins and strong FCF support the ~2× leverage; the risk is a deeper/longer housing downturn delaying the EBITDA recovery that the deleveraging path assumes.

3.5Returns↑

ROIC (adjusted NOPAT at a 25% normalized tax rate over equity + net debt) is ~13–14%, comfortably above a ~8.5% WACC — value-creating on an adjusted basis, though the Masonite impairment is a reminder that reported returns on the deal capital have been poor so far.

3.6Projections (FY2026E–FY2030E)↑

The base case assumes a soft 2026 (revenue −2%) giving way to a mid-single-digit housing/R&R recovery, with the adjusted-EBITDA margin rebuilding to a conservative 23.0% terminal (below management's mid-20s% ambition) on cost-out and Doors-margin normalization.

  • Revenue rises from ~$9.9bn (FY2026E) to ~$11.5bn (FY2030E); adjusted EBITDA from ~$2.08bn to ~$2.65bn.
  • Free cash flow builds from ~$0.82bn to ~$1.33bn (~$5.3bn cumulative over FY2026–30E), funding dividends, buybacks and debt paydown.
  • Net debt/EBITDA delevers from ~2.1× to ~1.0×, interest coverage rises to ~13×, and ROIC improves toward ~16% — the credit and equity stories both improve as the cycle turns.
  • Projections are driver-based off the Assumptions tab; the projected balance sheet balances and the projected cash flow foots in every year (QA: identities tie, balance = 0.00, plumbing ERR = 0).

3.7Guidance & catalysts↑

Management's FY2026 framing: revenue/earnings "largely aligned with consensus," enterprise adjusted-EBITDA margin building from mid-teens (1Q) toward the low-20s, capex ~$800m, interest $255–265m, tax 24–26%.

  • Q2-2026 guidance: revenue $2.6–2.7bn, adjusted-EBITDA margin 20–22%.
  • Near-term cost headwinds: ~$60m Iran-conflict-related inflation in Q2; tariffs broadly mitigated (~$10m Q1 net), with up to ~$25m of potential tariff refunds.
  • Catalysts: pace of the R&R/new-construction recovery, Doors-margin trajectory and synergy capture, capex normalization (FCF inflection), and continued shareholder returns ($2bn 2025–26 commitment).

4Valuation & Entry↑ Contents

Key takeaways

  • Fairly valued on its own history: the DCF base - $156/share on a 7.5x exit multiple (upper half of OC's realized 4.5-8.0x EV/EBITDA range) - sits level with the price; upside needs a multiple OC has not sustained (9-10x -> $190+) or an operational upturn (bull $216).
  • The peer discount is structural, not a catalyst: OC's 8.6x vs the ~14x peer median looks cheap, but OC has persistently traded several turns below these higher-quality, less-cyclical names - the gap reflects cyclicality, ~2.5x leverage and the Doors drag.
  • Value now, timing later: with intrinsic ~= price (no cushion) and the stock technically extended near its 52-week high (RSI 72, ~29% above a rising EMA100), the stance is Wait - a pullback toward ~$121-140 restores both a margin of safety and a cleaner entry.

4.1Overall Valuation Assessment↑

Owens Corning is Fair Value. On a DCF terminal anchored to OC's own through-cycle EV/EBITDA (a 7.5x exit, upper-half of its realized 4.5-8.0x range), intrinsic value is $156/share - essentially today's price. The headline discount to building-products peers (8.6x vs ~14x) is real but structural: OC has persistently traded several turns below the higher-quality, less-cyclical names, so it is not a re-rating catalyst. After the rally to 8.6x - the top of OC's five-year range - the stock prices in roughly its fair through-cycle multiple; the case for owning it now rests on cash quality and operational upside, not a valuation gap.

  • Intrinsic - DCF base (FCFF/EV, 7.5x exit) $156/share vs $156, ~0% upside; scenario range $86 (bear, 6.0x/downturn) - $216 (bull, 8.5x/upturn). A through-cycle-average multiple (~6-6.5x) implies ~$123-140 (below the price); 9-10x (above OC's history) is required for the $190+ zone.
  • Relative - OC's 8.6x EV/LTM EBITDA is ~38% below the peer median (13.9x) and ~41% below the core-comp median (14.6x); 13.1x NTM P/E is ~19% below the peer median. But OC's own history (4.5-8.0x) sits structurally below these peers - the discount reflects cyclicality, leverage and the Doors drag, and has been persistent.
  • FCF yield vs cost of capital - trailing FCF yield 6.6% is the highest in the peer set and +225bps over the 10-yr Treasury (4.37%); below the 8.5% WACC today on depressed FCF, rising toward it as capex normalizes. This is the most supportive leg.

Upside - Sector-high cash generation (6.6% FCF yield) plus self-funded deleveraging to ~1.0x by FY2030E - a quality compounder if the cycle cooperates. - Operational optionality: a Doors-margin recovery and a stronger housing/R&R upcycle drive the bull case (~$216). - Cheap on headline multiples vs peers - relevant only if the structural discount ever narrows.

Downside - At 8.6x, OC is at the top of its own 5-year range - limited multiple re-rating room; reversion toward ~6-7x implies $123-145 (downside to today's price). - Intrinsic value ~= price: no margin of safety, and the DCF is ~74% terminal value. - Cyclical/rate-sensitive downturn (bear ~$86) and an unproven Doors-margin recovery.

Valuation verdict: Fair Value - on OC's own through-cycle multiple the shares are priced about right; the peer discount is structural rather than a catalyst, and the genuine positives (FCF yield, deleveraging, operational upside) are largely reflected in the current 8.6x. (This supersedes the earlier "Attractive" read, which leaned on a ~9-10x terminal multiple OC has never sustained.)

OC is fairly valued on its own through-cycle economics - a $156 DCF base (7.5x exit EV/EBITDA, level with the price) - so there is no valuation cushion at current levels, and the shares are also technically extended (overbought, RSI 72, ~29% above a rising 100-day EMA near the 52-week high). With value neutral and timing poor, this is a Wait: prefer a pullback toward the EMA100 (~$121) / mid-band before building a position. The upside case ($216 bull) needs an operational upturn or multiple re-rating; the downside ($86 bear) is a cycle trough.

4.2Relative Valuation↑

OC trades at a wide discount to its building-products peer set on every earnings/cash-flow multiple - but the discount is structural and persistent, consistent with OC's own sub-8x trading history versus peers at 12-15x. Weight the core comps (CSL, FBIN, JELD); JELD-WEN is distressed and its multiples are non-meaningful (lean on medians).

CompanyPriceUS$Mkt capUS$MLTM RevUS$MLTM EBITDAUS$MEBITDA mgn%EV/EBITDAxNTM P/ExP/FCFxFCF yld%Div yld%
  • Read: OC is the cheapest healthy name on EV/EBITDA and below the peer-median P/E, with the highest FCF yield - but it has always traded here; the gap to CSL/MAS is structural, not an obvious mispricing.
  • EV/EBITDA Mean is distorted by JELD (37.5x) - use the medians.
  • The discount is not a margin story (OC's 21.1% Adj. margin tops the peer median); it reflects cyclicality, ~2.5x leverage and the Doors overhang.

Why the discount persists — structural, not a re-rating catalyst: - Commodity-cyclical mix: the profit pool is asphalt shingles + fiberglass insulation (crude/energy-linked, priced with a pass-through lag), which the market rates below branded, less-cyclical franchises such as Masco (R&R/consumer brands) and Carlisle (building envelope). - High operating leverage: 143 plants and a fixed-cost-heavy base make EBITDA swing with housing starts, rates and weather (Q4-2025 roofing shipments −27%); more-volatile cash flows are capitalized at lower multiples. - Capital intensity → weaker cash conversion: ~$800m capex (~6–8% of sales) leaves only ~40% of EBITDA converting to free cash, so a lower EV/EBITDA is partly rational versus asset-lighter peers. - Capital-allocation overhang: the $1.2bn Doors impairment ~18 months after the $3.9bn Masonite deal dents M&A credibility, and the Doors mix (21% of revenue, ~11% margin) dilutes group quality — the premium focused, well-allocated peers (e.g. Carlisle) earn has to be re-earned.

What could narrow it: a Doors-margin recovery (11% → mid-teens), deleveraging toward ~1.0x, and a return to clean GAAP earnings — each must be proven, so the discount is treated as a fact to live with, not a catalyst.

4.2.1Data caveats↑

  • Core comps (CSL, FBIN, JELD) - weight these; lean on the medians (read-across names pull the mean).
  • JELD: distressed (1.2% margin, negative FCF) -> EV/EBITDA outlier, P/E & P/FCF n/m; excluded from the football-field bars.
  • EBITDA basis: subject Adjusted vs peers reported - modestly flatters OC's relative multiple.
  • Net-debt basis: subject net debt $5,235mm is the S3 model LTM (1Q26, seasonally elevated); peers Yahoo. All USD.

4.3Intrinsic Valuation↑

The step-4 dual DCF, with the terminal set to a 7.5x exit EV/EBITDA (upper half of OC's own 4.5-8.0x history), values OC at $156/share - level with the price. The levered FCFE cross-check ($152) is within ~3%, so the two methods corroborate a ~$152-156 equity value. Figures are narrated from the engine artifacts; nothing is recomputed here.

  • Bridge: PV explicit FCFF $4,593mm + PV terminal $13,227mm = EV $17,820mm; less net debt $5,235mm = equity $12,585mm / 80.5mm = $156.33/share. Implied EV ~= 8.6x FY2026E EBITDA - where OC trades today.
  • Scenario range: Bear $86 - Base $156 - Bull $216 - exit multiples 6.0x / 7.5x / 8.5x (all within/at OC's history) combined with downturn / Gate-B / upturn operating paths and 9.0% / 8.5% / 8.0% WACC.
  • Terminal method: exit multiple 7.5x (TV = 74.2% of EV); implies ~1.3% perpetuity growth. The Gordon g=2.5% case (~=9.1x implied exit) gives $192 but assumes a multiple above OC's realized range - shown only for comparison.
  • WACC 8.5% = 11.05% Ke (Rf 4.3% + b1.35 x 5.0% ERP) and 3.75% after-tax Kd at a 65/35 market structure.
  • Diagnostics: FCFF-FCFE gap just 2.7% (the legs converge once the terminal sits at OC's own multiple); the value is ~74% terminal, so it is sensitive to the exit assumption - see the stress test.

4.4Football Field↑

Triangulating the methods against the price: the DCF midpoint ($156) sits right on the current price, while the comp-implied bars ($166-408) sit above - the spread is OC's structural discount to higher-multiple peers, not a separate upside signal.

Method Low Mid High
DCF (FCFF) 85.55 156.33 215.90
EV/EBITDA Comps 214.80 275.44 407.74
NTM P/E Comps 165.58 191.80 254.44
52-Week Range (market reference) 97.53 - 159.42
Current price 156.05
  • Convergence: the DCF (anchored to OC's own multiple) lands at the price; the comps bars sit a tier higher because peers trade at 12-15x vs OC's sub-9x - the methods diverge by design, and the gap is the structural discount.
  • The current price is at the top of the 52-week range ($98-159) and at the DCF midpoint - fair, not cheap, on intrinsic.
  • Basis/scope: EV/EBITDA comps apply peer reported-EBITDA multiples to OC's Adjusted EBITDA (upward bias); JELD-WEN excluded from the bars as distressed/n-m.

4.5Valuation Stress Test↑

The value is most sensitive to the terminal assumption and WACC. Both grids below use the same orientation — the terminal parameter (exit multiple / growth) in rows, WACC in columns — and sit side by side: Grid 1 is the primary (exit multiple); Grid 2 is the perpetuity-growth (Gordon) equivalent for comparison. Base cells in bold.

Grid 1 — exit multiple × WACC (primary; base 7.5x / 8.5%)

Exit ＼ WACC8.0%8.5%9.0%
6.0x127.31123.43119.65
7.0x149.72145.33141.06
7.5x160.93156.33151.76
8.0x172.14167.24162.46
9.0x194.55189.14183.87

Grid 2 — perpetuity growth × WACC (Gordon; base g 2.5% / 8.5%)

g ＼ WACC8.0%8.5%9.0%
1.5%180.30162.12146.37
2.0%196.94176.12158.28
2.5%216.60192.45172.02
3.0%240.20211.75188.06
3.5%269.05234.92207.01
  • The two grids reconcile via the implied-growth cross-check: the 7.5x exit base ($156) corresponds to ~1.3% perpetuity growth, while the Gordon g=2.5% assumption (Grid 2 centre, $192) corresponds to a ~9.1x implied exit. The ~$36 gap between the two "base" cells is entirely the terminal-multiple-vs-growth assumption.
  • Reverse-DCF: at the base 8.5% WACC, the current ~$156 price implies a ~7.5x exit multiple (Grid 1) - squarely OC's upper-half historical multiple - or equivalently a ~1.3% perpetuity growth (below Grid 2's top g-row of 1.5% in the 8.5% column). Either way the market is paying for OC's own through-cycle economics, no re-rating embedded.
  • Terminal-value reliance: ~74% of EV sits in the terminal value; the bear case ($86) captures a cycle trough where the multiple compresses to 6x and operations weaken together.

4.6Technical Analysis↑

Technical read as of 2026-06-29. OC is in a confirmed uptrend but technically stretched - price sits ~29% above a rising 100-day EMA and is overbought (RSI 72, Bollinger %B 1.35) after a rally to near its 52-week high - so the read is to wait for a pullback rather than chase at current levels.

  • Trend: price $156.05 is +28.8% above a rising EMA100 (~$121, slope +2.0%) - price and the long-term trend agree (uptrend), but the gap to the EMA is wide.
  • Momentum: RSI(20) 72 - overbought (>70); MACD histogram +2.70 - supportive (>0). Momentum is strong but extended, raising near-term mean-reversion risk.
  • Volatility / bands: Bollinger %B 1.35 - above the upper 2-sigma band; trades beyond the band typically resolve into consolidation or a pullback to the 20-day mid-band.
  • Volume: ~379% of the 20-day average on the up-move - strong trend confirmation (no distribution), though such a spike can also mark a near-climactic push.
  • Key levels: resistance ~$159 (52-week high, being tested); support ~$121 (rising EMA100 - the primary pullback/entry target) and ~$98 (52-week low).
  • Setup: trend intact but stretched - the cleaner entry is a pullback toward the EMA100 / mid-band, not the current overbought level.

4.6.1Entry Assessment (combined)↑

OC is fairly valued on its own through-cycle economics - a $156 DCF base (7.5x exit EV/EBITDA, level with the price) - so there is no valuation cushion at current levels, and the shares are also technically extended: overbought (RSI 72, %B 1.35) and ~29% above a rising 100-day EMA near the 52-week high. With value neutral and timing poor, this is a Wait - prefer a pullback toward the EMA100 (~$121) / mid-band, which would also restore an intrinsic margin of safety below the $156 base. (The earlier "Attractive" read leaned on a ~9-10x terminal multiple OC has not sustained; on a history-anchored 7.5x exit the stock is fair, not cheap.)

Preferred entry zone: ~$121-140 (pullback toward the rising EMA100; restores both a margin of safety below the $156 base and a cleaner technical entry)

Key levels: | Level | Price | Basis | |---|---|---| | Resistance 1 | ~$159 | 52-week high - price testing it now | | Support 1 | ~$121 | Rising 100-day EMA (~29% below spot) - primary pullback / cleaner-entry target | | Support 2 | ~$98 | 52-week low - cycle trough / deep support |

Technical read as of 2026-06-29. Technical analysis presented as research illustration only.

5Appendix↑ Contents

5.1Financials — Basis of Preparation↑

Reporting currency USD, $ millions, US GAAP, fiscal-year end December 31, quarterly cadence. Headline non-GAAP measures are the company-reported Adjusted EBITDA and Adjusted diluted EPS; Adjusted operating income is analyst-derived (Adjusted EBITDA less D&A). LTM = trailing twelve months through 1Q26 = FY2025 + 1Q26 − 1Q25. The historical timeline carries TWO bases, broken at FY2023→FY2024: (i) FY2021–FY2023 are AS-REPORTED TOTAL-COMPANY (pre-recast, including the former Composites / Glass Reinforcements business and pre-Masonite), with the FULL income statement, cash flow statement and balance sheet as originally reported (company Adjusted EBITDA $1,904m / $2,267m / $2,313m respectively); only the current three-segment split is not shown for those years, as the company never restated segments that far back; (ii) FY2024–FY2025 (and interims 1Q25/1Q26, LTM, projections) are RECAST CONTINUING OPERATIONS (ex-GR, post-Masonite; three segments Roofing/Insulation/Doors). The FY2023→FY2024 step reflects the Masonite acquisition (Doors added, May 2024) and the GR divestiture/discontinued-operations recast (Composites removed; GR sold 30 April 2026 for ~$280m) and is not like-for-like. The company recast only two years (FY2024–FY2025) of segment and Adjusted EBITDA in the FY2025 10-K, so a continuing-ops FY2023 figure was never published. Segment profit metric = company-reported segment EBITDA. FY2026E–FY2030E columns are model estimates.

5.2Key Assumptions & Methodology↑

5.2.1Financial Model Assumptions↑

Base-case projection drivers (FY2026E–FY2030E), signed off at CP3. FY2026 anchored to company guidance; the margin path was set conservatively at the analyst's instruction to a 23.0% terminal Adjusted-EBITDA margin (below management's mid-20s% long-term ambition). Projections are computed (balanced, circular-free): interest on opening debt, cash/debt as the balancing item, retained-earnings roll. Labelled sourced (guidance/transcript/filing) vs assumed (analyst).

Assumption Value Basis Source
Revenue growth FY26 −2.0%, then +3.5%/+4.5%/+4.0%/+3.5% Q1-26 actual −10% + Q2 guide; housing/R&R recovery assumed
Adj. EBITDA margin path 21.0% → 23.0% terminal (FY30E) FY26 guidance anchor; conservative ramp per CP3 assumed
Capex $800m FY26, normalizing to ~$715m (~6.2% of sales) ~$800m FY26 guidance sourced
D&A ~$680m FY26, rising to $760m ~$680m FY26 guidance sourced
Interest expense $260m FY26, declining to $205m $255–265m FY26 guidance; declines on paydown sourced
Tax rate 25% guided 24–26% midpoint sourced
Working-capital days DSO 34 / DIO 74 / DPO 63 (held flat) FY2025 actuals assumed
Dividends / buybacks $255m→$325m div; ~$400–450m/yr buyback $2bn 2025–26 shareholder-return commitment; 15% dividend raise sourced
Debt paydown $300m/yr (FCF-funded) deleveraging path assumed
GR divestiture proceeds ~$280m cash in FY2026 closed 30 Apr 2026 (Q1-2026 release) sourced
WACC (S4 DCF) 8.5% Rf 4.3% + β1.35 × ERP 5.0% → Ke ~11.1%; Kd ~5.0% pre-tax; ~35% market D/V assumed
Terminal method (S4, final) Exit multiple 7.5x EV/EBITDA upper half of OC's realized 4.5–8.0x history (≈1.3% implied g) assumed
OC through-cycle EV/EBITDA 4.5–8.0x (avg ~6.2x); 8.6x today computed from FY2021–25 year-ends — corrects the earlier "~10x median" placeholder sourced

5.2.2Comps & Relative-Valuation Methodology↑

Peer set: CSL, FBIN, JELD (core — direct roofing-envelope/doors reads) + MAS, LPX, EXP (read-across U.S. building-products). Every multiple is computed from raw inputs (EV = market cap + net debt; EV/EBITDA, NTM P/E, P/FCF, div & FCF yield) — no vendor multiples lifted. Sourcing: subject financials from the S3 model LTM on a management Adjusted-EBITDA basis (Revenue 9,838; Adj. EBITDA 2,072; net debt 5,235; FCF 827; dividends 236 $mm), subject market data and all peer data from Yahoo Finance live (2026-06-29) on a reported-EBITDA basis. The Adjusted-vs-reported asymmetry modestly flatters OC's relative margin/multiple. Stats are over peers only (subject excluded); Core-comp rows over the tier=core peers. JELD-WEN is distressed (1.2% EBITDA margin, negative FCF): its EV/EBITDA is an outlier and its P/E & P/FCF are n/m, so medians are used and JELD is excluded from the football-field implied-value bars. LPX/EXP trailing FCF is weak (P/FCF n/m / elevated). All figures USD; no FX conversion required (all peers USD-listed).

5.3DCF Assumptions, WACC Build & Output Detail↑

Dual FCFF + FCFE DCF (5-yr explicit window FY2026E–FY2030E reproducing the Gate-B model UFCF, continuing-operations basis). Terminal = exit multiple 7.5x EV/EBITDA (analyst choice; upper half of OC's realized 4.5–8.0x history, 8.6x today; implies ~1.3% perpetuity growth). EV→equity bridge: PV explicit FCFF 4,593 + PV terminal 13,227 = EV 17,820; less net debt 5,235 = equity 12,585 / 80.5mm sh = $156.33 (FCFF) — level with the $156 price → Fair Value. FCFE cross-check $152.14 (gap only 2.7% once the terminal sits at OC's own multiple). Terminal value 74.2% of EV. Scenarios (exit-multiple based): Bear $85.55 (6.0x, downturn, 9.0% WACC) / Base $156.33 (7.5x, 8.5%) / Bull $215.90 (8.5x, upturn, 8.0%). Two stress grids retained — exit-multiple × WACC (primary) and perpetuity-g × WACC (Gordon) — see the Valuation Stress Test. Reverse-DCF: $156 ⇒ ~7.5x exit or ~1.3% perpetuity g at 8.5% WACC. Correction: the prior Gate-B "~10x through-cycle median" / Gordon g=2.5% (9.1x-implied, $192) overstated value — OC has never closed a year near 10x.

5.4Peer Set Construction↑

Ticker Name Tier Rationale Basis notes
CSL Carlisle Companies core Closest read on OC's high-margin Roofing/insulation envelope franchise; similar margin/consolidation dynamics Commercial-roofing weighted vs OC residential tilt; reported EBITDA (Yahoo)
FBIN Fortune Brands Innovations core Owns Therma-Tru doors — direct read on OC's Doors segment + branded R&R/new-construction Water/security/outdoors mix; reported EBITDA (Yahoo)
JELD JELD-WEN Holding core Most direct public comp for the Doors (Masonite) segment Distressed/sub-scale — multiples n/m; excluded from football-field bars; reported EBITDA (Yahoo)
MAS Masco broader Best read-across on branded building-products economics and U.S. R&R demand Different categories (paint, plumbing); ~85% R&R; reported EBITDA (Yahoo)
LPX Louisiana-Pacific broader Siding + OSB; strong read on U.S. new-residential cyclicality and capital intensity Commodity OSB earnings volatility; weak trailing FCF; reported EBITDA (Yahoo)
EXP Eagle Materials broader High-margin, U.S. housing-levered heavy-side building materials (wallboard + cement) Heavy-side model, no direct product overlap; reported EBITDA (Yahoo)

5.5Data Gaps & Open Items↑

Item Why it matters Where flagged
Customer concentration (top-customer / top-10 %) Revenue durability and buyer bargaining power vs. big-box and two-step distribution Company Overview · Customers / Sales & Distribution
Masonite implied EBITDA and precise purchase multiple Tests whether the ~$3.9bn deal (since impaired $1.2bn) was over-paid Company Overview · Key Acquisitions and Divestitures
Recast segment EBITDA for FY2021–FY2023 Needed for a clean 5-year segment model; only FY2024–FY2025 disclosed on the current three-segment basis Company Overview · Key Reporting Segments
Fixed vs. variable cost split Sizes operating leverage and downside margin in a volume downturn Company Overview · Cost Drivers
Upcoming CBA renegotiations / recent labor disputes 61% of hourly employees are unionized — wage and disruption risk Company Overview · Employees
Independent chair / lead-director structure Governance quality — Chair and CEO roles are combined Company Overview · Management Overview
Named key suppliers / identity of sole-source Roofing input Supply-chain concentration risk (sole-source flagged but unnamed) Company Overview · Suppliers
OC precise market share by sub-segment Sizes the competitive moat; only company estimates exist (#2 roofing, #1 NA fiberglass) Industry Overview · Competitive Landscape & Positioning
CR4 / HHI by sub-market Quantifies consolidation and pricing discipline Industry Overview · Industry Structure & Value Chain
U.S.-specific (vs. global) fiberglass-insulation market size Third-party figures conflate U.S./global scope and product definitions Industry Overview · Industry Definition, Size & Characteristics

6Disclosures↑ Contents

Analyst certification. The views expressed in this report accurately reflect the analyst's personal views about the subject company and its securities; no part of the analyst's compensation was, is, or will be directly or indirectly related to the specific recommendations or views expressed herein.

Rating definitions (entry scale — no price target). This report uses a three-point valuation scale rather than a price target: Attractive — intrinsic value and relative multiples imply a favourable risk/reward versus the current price; Fair Value — the shares are broadly priced in line with intrinsic and relative value; Expensive — valuation implies an unfavourable risk/reward. Entry timing (accumulate / starter buy / wait / avoid adding) is a separate technical overlay and does not change the valuation rating.

Important disclosures. This is an initiating-coverage report prepared for research illustration. Valuation is derived from a discounted-cash-flow model and a peer-multiple comparison; all figures are computed from company filings and publicly available market data as of 2026-06-29. Subject EBITDA is management-Adjusted; peer EBITDA is reported. The technical analysis is presented as a research illustration only.

Disclaimer. This material is for informational purposes only and does not constitute investment advice, an offer, or a solicitation to buy or sell any security. It is not tailored to any individual's circumstances. Forward-looking estimates are subject to material uncertainty and may differ from actual results. Past performance and technical signals are not reliable indicators of future results. No representation is made as to the accuracy or completeness of third-party data.

Sources & notes

Initiating-coverage research compiled from the company's filings, the data room and the analysis sections. Figures are as stated in the underlying sections. Not investment advice.
